text: Apex Systems Budget Policy <DATE_TIME> — Effective <DATE_TIME> This policy defines how <DATE_TIME> and <DATE_TIME> budgets are constructed, approved, managed, and monitored at Apex Systems. It applies to every cost center owner, functional leader, finance business partner, and any employee with purchase authority. Understanding this policy is a prerequisite for any employee who manages a budget line, approves vendor contracts, or initiates headcount requests. Budget Cycle and Calendar <DATE_TIME> at Apex runs from <DATE_TIME>. Budget planning follows a structured annual calendar designed to give functional leaders enough time to build thoughtful plans while giving finance enough time to consolidate, stress-test, and present a credible plan to the board. <DATE_TIME>: The CFO issues top-down guidance to each functional leader. The guidance specifies the target revenue growth rate for <DATE_TIME>, the total headcount budget by function, and the maximum operating expense envelope. It is not a blank check for functional leaders to fill — it is a constraint to plan within. <DATE_TIME>: Functional leaders conduct internal planning sessions. Each leader works with their finance business partner to identify the investments required to meet the revenue and efficiency targets. Decisions made during this window should be grounded in concrete business cases, not aspirational wish lists. <DATE_TIME>: Functional leaders submit their bottoms-up plans to the finance business partner. Plans are submitted in the standardized budget template, which includes: personnel cost by level and role, software and tools cost by vendor, professional services, facilities and travel, and a narrative memo explaining how the investments connect to company objectives. <DATE_TIME>: Finance consolidates the submissions, identifies overlaps and gaps, runs sensitivity analyses, and produces the integrated company plan. Finance schedules individual working sessions with each functional leader to reconcile where the bottoms-up plan exceeds the top-down envelope. <DATE_TIME>: The senior leadership team reviews the integrated plan in two sessions, one focused on revenue and go-to-market, one focused on operating expense and headcount. Material trade-offs are surfaced for CEO-level decision. <DATE_TIME> board meeting: The board of directors reviews and approves the final <DATE_TIME> plan, including the total headcount target, the revenue plan, the operating expense budget, and the capital expenditure budget. <DATE_TIME>: Functional leaders receive their approved cost center budgets and are expected to communicate the plan to their teams before <DATE_TIME>. <DATE_TIME> Reforecasts In addition to the <DATE_TIME> plan, Apex produces two formal reforecasts per year. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> reforecast (<DATE_TIME>): Based on actual results through <DATE_TIME> and a revised assessment of <DATE_TIME> performance, the <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> reforecast updates the <DATE_TIME> outlook for revenue, operating expense, headcount additions, and ending cash balance. The <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> reforecast may result in approved budget adjustments for functions that are tracking significantly ahead of or behind plan. <LOCATION> reforecast (<DATE_TIME>): The <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> reforecast is the last opportunity to revise the <DATE_TIME> outlook before the <DATE_TIME> planning cycle begins again. It also serves as a first look at <DATE_TIME>, providing early signals about where investment levels should increase or decrease. Both reforecast submissions are due from functional leaders within <DATE_TIME> of the reforecast kickoff date communicated by Finance. Submissions that are late or incomplete are escalated to the

Budget Structure and Coding Budgets are organized by cost center. Each cost center is a six-digit code assigned by Finance and corresponds to a single functional team with one designated owner and one finance business partner. Cost center owners are responsible for all spending within their center. Expenses are classified by natural account, which identifies the type of expense: 6100 — Personnel (salaries, bonus, benefits, payroll taxes) 6200 — Software and SaaS subscriptions 6300 — Professional services and consulting 6400 — Facilities and utilities 6500 — Marketing and demand generation 6600 — Travel and entertainment 6700 — Other operating expenses Every expense transaction committed in the accounting system must carry a cost center code, a natural account code, and, for project-tracked spend, a project code assigned by Finance. Transactions missing required codes are returned to the submitter; vendors are not paid until coding is complete. Capitalized Versus Operating Expense Classification Software developed for internal use must be assessed for capitalization eligibility under ASC 350-40. A project is eligible for capitalization when three conditions are met: the project has entered the application development stage (preliminary project activities, including evaluation and selection of alternatives, are complete), the project has a defined scope and estimated budget, and management has authorized and committed resources to completing the project. Costs eligible for capitalization include external consulting fees for development work, employee salaries for time directly attributable to development, and interest during development. Costs that are not eligible include training, data migration, maintenance, and overhead allocations. The Finance team maintains a capitalization register for all active projects. Cost center owners must notify Finance when a project begins the application development stage, so that capitalization can begin from the correct date. The capitalization register is reviewed <DATE_TIME>; projects that are no longer in active development are closed and no further costs are capitalized. Cloud infrastructure costs, third-party SaaS subscriptions regardless of contract length, and routine maintenance are expensed as incurred. Prepaid software licenses <DATE_TIME> are recognized on a straight-line amortization schedule. Headcount Planning and Approval Headcount is the largest single component of Apex's operating expense, typically representing 65% to 70% of total operating costs. Query planning is enhanced by a cost-based optimizer that is calibrated <DATE_TIME>. The calibration job samples recent query history, measures actual execution statistics, and updates table statistics stored in the Glue catalog. This keeps the optimizer's cardinality estimates accurate as data volumes grow. The query proxy service sits in front of <LOCATION> and enforces per-tenant query concurrency limits, maximum query execution time (<DATE_TIME>), and circuit breakers that reject queries when the cluster utilization exceeds 90%. Long-running queries that exceed the time limit are terminated with an informative error; customers are directed to the asynchronous jobs endpoint for analytics that require <DATE_TIME>. A Redis-backed result cache stores the outputs of deterministic queries — those with no volatile functions and no row-level security filters that vary by user — for a configurable TTL defined per tenant. Cache keys incorporate the query text, the table version identifier, and the tenant_id. A cache hit for a dashboard tile delivers the result in under 50 milliseconds. Control Plane The control plane is a Go-based service that manages the cross-cutting concerns of the entire platform: tenant provisioning, user management, role definitions, API key issuance, billing metering, audit log aggregation, and system health dashboards. It is the system of record for all configuration state. The control plane is backed by Aurora PostgreSQL with multi-AZ failover. The primary instance handles all writes; read replicas in the same region serve read-heavy operations such as user lookup and API key validation. The Aurora cluster is sized at db.r6g.xlarge with storage autoscaling enabled. A connection pool managed by PgBouncer keeps the number of database connections bounded regardless of the number of application pods. All write operations to the control plane produce an audit event containing the actor, the resource type and ID, the action, the before and after state, a timestamp, and a cryptographic hash of the event chain. Events are signed and appended to an immutable CloudWatch Logs stream. Customers with Enterprise contracts have read access to their own audit log stream and may export it to their <LOCATION>. Authentication and Authorization Authentication uses OpenID Connect. Supported identity providers are Okta, Microsoft Azure Active Directory, and Google Workspace. Customers configure their identity provider through the control plane UI; the <LOCATION> service registers as an OIDC relying party and validates tokens against the provider's discovery endpoint. Employees and internal service accounts authenticate through Okta. Authorization is enforced by an Open Policy Agent sidecar co-deployed with every service that handles customer data. The OPA instance receives the JWT from the incoming request and evaluates the request against the current policy bundle, which includes three built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and any custom roles defined by the tenant. Policy bundles are distributed by a bundle server and refreshed every <DATE_TIME>. JWTs issued to customer users are valid for <DATE_TIME>. Refresh tokens are stored as HTTP-only cookies with a <DATE_TIME> lifetime and are rotated on each use. API keys are long-lived bearer

Professional services revenue represents implementation, configuration, and training services contracted separately from the platform subscription. Revenue is recognized as services are delivered using an input-based measure of progress, measured by <DATE_TIME> incurred relative to total estimated hours. Usage-based revenue above a committed minimum is recognized in the period consumed. Usage that exceeds the committed contractual minimum triggers an overage invoice within <DATE_TIME>. Contract modifications are evaluated to determine whether they constitute a termination of the existing contract combined with a new contract, a prospective change, or a cumulative catch-up adjustment. The Revenue Operations team consults with Finance on each modification that changes the total transaction price by more than $50,000. Deferred Revenue and Billings The deferred revenue balance represents amounts invoiced to customers for services not yet delivered. Deferred revenue is the single largest liability on the Apex balance sheet and is reviewed in detail at <DATE_TIME> by the external auditors. The deferred revenue subledger tracks each contract individually, with the original contract start date, the contract end date, the total transaction price, the cumulative amount recognized, and the remaining deferred balance. The subledger is reconciled to the general ledger daily; any reconciling item is investigated and resolved before <DATE_TIME>. Billings — the total value of invoices issued in a period — is a key operating metric tracked separately from recognized revenue. Billings growth reflects new sales momentum and renewal health. The difference between billings and revenue in any period represents the change in deferred revenue. FP&A monitors the billings-to-revenue ratio <DATE_TIME> as a leading indicator of future revenue growth. Key Performance Indicators and Metrics Apex tracks both GAAP financial metrics and <NRP> operating metrics. All metric definitions are documented in the metrics dictionary maintained by FP&A. Changes to any definition require CFO approval and must be disclosed in reporting materials with a note identifying the period in which the change took effect and the impact of the change on prior periods. GAAP metrics: total revenue, gross profit, gross margin percentage, operating expense by natural account, operating income (loss), net income (loss), and cash and cash equivalents. Non-GAAP operating metrics: <DATE_TIME> recurring revenue (ARR), defined as the annualized value of all active subscription contracts as of the measurement date, excluding one-time fees and services. Net new ARR, the change in ARR from one period to the next. Gross dollar retention (<LOCATION>), the percentage of beginning ARR retained from existing customers excluding expansions, measured <DATE_TIME>. Net dollar retention (NDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained including expansions and contractions from existing customers, measured <DATE_TIME>. Customer acquisition cost (CAC), total sales and marketing expense divided by the number of new customers acquired. Magic number, the net new ARR generated per dollar

POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files Creates a pre-signed <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> PUT URL for uploading a file directly to Apex storage without passing through the API servers. Accepted file formats are Parquet, CSV (UTF-8 with configurable delimiter), and newline-delimited JSON. Maximum file size is 5 GB. The request body specifies the filename, format, and expected row count (optional, used for progress reporting). The response contains the pre-signed URL (valid for <DATE_TIME>), an upload_id, and the expected Content-Type header to use in the PUT request. After the file is uploaded to the pre-signed URL, the file must be registered by calling POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/complete. This triggers ingestion processing. Progress can be monitored through GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/status. GET /submissions/{submission_id} Returns the current status of a batch ingestion submission. Possible statuses are pending, processing, complete, and failed. A complete response includes the total record count, the accepted count, and the rejected count. A failed response includes a top-level error describing the systemic failure. Schema management: GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema returns the current schema version, column definitions, and validation rules. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema creates a new schema version. Schema versions are immutable once a dataset has data written under them; older versions continue to support ingestion until explicitly deprecated by the dataset owner. <PERSON>POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/query Executes a SQL query against the dataset and streams results using server-sent events (SSE). The request body contains a sql string (maximum 64 KB) and an optional params object for parameterized values. Parameterization is strongly recommended to prevent SQL injection and improve query plan caching. The SSE stream emits one JSON event per row produced by the query, a progress event <DATE_TIME> for long-running queries, and a final statistics event containing the row count, bytes scanned, execution duration, and cache hit indicator. The stream closes after the statistics event or on error. Queries are subject to a maximum execution time of <DATE_TIME>; queries that exceed this threshold are terminated and the client receives an error event with the reason execution_timeout. GET /jobs For queries expected to exceed <DATE_TIME>, use the asynchronous jobs endpoint. POST /jobs accepts the same request body as POST /query. The response returns a job_id and an estimated duration. The job runs in the background; results are written to a temporary <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> location accessible via GET /jobs/{job_id}/results. Jobs expire <DATE_TIME> after completion. Filtering and projection: To avoid transferring unnecessary columns, include a select array of column names in the query request. Server-side projection reduces response size and latency significantly for wide tables. Administration Endpoints User management: POST /users creates a user in the tenant with a specified role. The request body includes email, role, and an optional display_name. A welcome email with a credential setup link is sent automatically. PATCH /users/{user_id} updates a user's role or active status. DELETE /users/{user_id} deactivates the user and revokes all active sessions; the user record is retained for audit purposes. Default limits apply to all tenants unless overridden by a plan-level or contract-level configuration. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60 requests in <DATE_TIME>. Ingestion endpoints have a separate limit of 100 requests per minute to protect downstream <PERSON> throughput. Query endpoints have a concurrency limit of 10 simultaneous in-flight queries per tenant. Enterprise plan limits: 3,000 requests per minute and 200 simultaneous queries. Higher limits are available with advance notice; contact your account manager. When the rate limit is reached, the API returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. The response includes three headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (the configured limit), X-RateLimit-Remaining (the number of requests remaining in the current window), and Retry-After (the number of <DATE_TIME> until the limit resets). Clients must respect the Retry-After value. Ignoring the retry guidance and polling at high frequency during a rate-limit period may result in temporary IP-level blocking. All SDKs maintained by Apex implement exponential backoff with full jitter starting from the Retry-After value. Third-party integrations should implement the same. Error Model All error responses conform to RFC 7807 Problem Details. The response body is a JSON object with the following fields: type: a URI that identifies the error class, such as https://api.apex-systems.example/errors/schema-validation-failed. These URIs are stable and may be used in client code for programmatic error handling. title: a human-readable summary of the problem type, consistent for all occurrences of the same type. status: the HTTP status code as an integer, included for convenience. detail: a human-readable description of the specific occurrence, including actionable guidance where applicable. instance: a URI that identifies the specific request, useful for tracing in logs. correlation_id: a UUID generated per request. Customers should include this in support tickets. Apex support uses it to locate the request trace in <LOCATION>. For validation errors, an additional errors array is included, where each element identifies the field path that failed validation and the rule that was violated. Ingestion Endpoints POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/records Synchronously validates and asynchronously ingests a batch of JSON records. The request body is a JSON object with a records array of up to 1,000 individual records. Each record is validated against the dataset schema; records that fail validation are rejected and included in the error response without affecting the valid records in the same batch. The maximum size per record is 1 MB; the maximum total request body size is 50 MB. The response returns HTTP 202 Accepted with a body containing a <NRP> (UUID), a submitted_count, a rejected_count, and an array of rejection details. The submission is processed asynchronously; the submission status can be polled using GET /submissions/{submission_id}. law. Parental Leave Apex provides <DATE_TIME> of fully paid parental leave for the primary caregiver and <DATE_TIME> of fully paid parental leave for the secondary caregiver. These benefits apply to the birth, adoption, or foster placement of a child and to both biological and adoptive parents. Leave may be taken in a single continuous block or split into a maximum of two separate blocks within <DATE_TIME> of the qualifying event. Employees must notify their manager and HR at least 30 days before the intended start date when the timing is foreseeable. For birth events, notification at the time of awareness is expected; the <DATE_TIME> requirement is waived if the birth occurs earlier than anticipated. During parental leave, Apex continues all benefits at the normal employee premium contribution rate. Parental leave runs concurrently with any federal Family and Medical Leave Act entitlements. Employees who have worked at Apex for <DATE_TIME> or <DATE_TIME> in <DATE_TIME> are not eligible for FMLA protection but remain eligible for paid parental leave. The returning employee has the right to the same position or an equivalent position with the same base pay, bonus opportunity, and equity. Apex does not permit role changes, team transfers, or reporting structure changes initiated by the company during an employee's parental leave without the employee's written consent. Managers may not contact employees on parental leave for work-related matters except in documented emergencies with HR Business Partner approval. Employees who voluntarily maintain contact with the team during leave do so at their own discretion. Bereavement Leave Employees receive up to 5 paid <DATE_TIME> of bereavement leave for the loss of a spouse, domestic partner, child, parent, sibling, or grandparent. Employees receive up to 3 paid days for the loss of an in-law, step-parent, step-sibling, or step-child. Employees receive up to 1 paid day for the loss of a close friend or colleague at the manager's discretion. <PERSON> leave may be taken non-consecutively within <DATE_TIME> of the death to accommodate travel, services, or estate obligations. Proof of death, such as an obituary or death certificate, may be requested for absences of <DATE_TIME>. Additional unpaid leave beyond these limits may be requested through the HR Business Partner and is evaluated case by case with consideration for the employee's circumstances, team capacity, and business needs. Jury Duty and Civic Obligations Apex pays full base salary for employees called to jury duty or subpoenaed as a witness in a legal proceeding unrelated to Apex business. There is no cap on <DATE_TIME> covered. The employee must submit a copy of the court summons to the payroll team through the HR portal <DATE_TIME> of service. Any payment received from the court for jury service may be retained by the employee. Employees are expected to report to work on <DATE_TIME> when they are released from jury duty before <DATE_TIME>. On <DATE_TIME> they are dismissed <DATE_TIME>, they are expected to work for the remainder of the <DATE_TIME> if feasible. Military leave is provided in accordance with the Uniformed Services Employment and Reemployment Rights Act. Employees returning from qualifying military service are reinstated to

through the HR portal. Urgent payroll corrections are resolved within <DATE_TIME>; non-urgent adjustments appear on the next regular pay date. Compensation Confidentiality Compensation information is treated as confidential between the employee, their manager, HR, and the finance team. Employees are discouraged from sharing specific compensation details with colleagues, not because Apex prohibits it — federal law protects the right to discuss wages — but because comparison without full context often creates misunderstanding. Retaliation against any employee for discussing compensation is prohibited and will be treated as a violation of the code of conduct.

Apex Systems Leave Policy Effective <DATE_TIME> This document defines all categories of paid and unpaid leave available to Apex Systems employees. It applies to all regular full-time employees based in <LOCATION>. Employees working outside <LOCATION> should consult the local addendum maintained by the HR Business Partner for their region, which may provide different or additional entitlements required by local law. Paid Time Off Full-time employees accrue PTO at the rate of <DATE_TIME> per calendar month, for a total of <DATE_TIME> per year. Accrual begins on <DATE_TIME> of employment and is credited at the start of <DATE_TIME>. Part-time employees working at least 20 hours per week accrue PTO on a prorated basis. PTO encompasses vacation, personal days, and any other discretionary time away from work that does not fall into one of the specific categories below. Employees are encouraged to use their PTO throughout <DATE_TIME> to maintain work-life balance. Managers are responsible for proactively planning coverage to ensure employees can take leave without jeopardizing customer commitments. PTO may be taken in minimum increments of <DATE_TIME>. Requests of <DATE_TIME> require at least three business days of advance notice. Requests of <DATE_TIME> require manager approval submitted through the HR portal <DATE_TIME> before the intended start date. Up to five unused PTO days may be carried over into <DATE_TIME>. Any accrual above the <DATE_TIME> carryover cap is forfeited on <DATE_TIME>. Employees who were unable to use PTO due to business-critical needs may apply to their HR Business Partner for an exception carryover of up to five additional days, subject to VP approval. Employees leaving the company are paid out for all accrued but unused PTO in their final paycheck, subject to the laws of the employee's home state. <LOCATION> employees are paid out at 100% regardless of any forfeiture provisions above; other states vary. <PERSON> leave is provided separately from PTO at <DATE_TIME> per year, front-loaded on <DATE_TIME>. New hires receive a prorated sick leave balance on their start date based on the number of <DATE_TIME> remaining in <DATE_TIME>. Sick leave may be used for: personal illness or injury, medical or dental appointments, mental health days, preventive care, and care for an immediate family member who is ill, injured, or attending a medical appointment. Apex Systems Compensation Guide Effective <DATE_TIME> This guide explains how Apex Systems structures base pay, variable compensation, equity, and benefits for all full-time employees. It is distributed at hire, republished <DATE_TIME> after the <DATE_TIME> compensation review, and updated <DATE_TIME> if material changes are made. Managers are encouraged to walk through this guide with new team members during <DATE_TIME> of employment. Compensation Philosophy Apex targets the 65th percentile of base salary for our peer group, defined as venture-backed enterprise software companies with between 200 and 2,000 employees and <DATE_TIME> recurring revenue between $50 million and $500 million, primarily based in <LOCATION>. Total target compensation — including base salary, target <DATE_TIME> bonus, and the annualized Black-Scholes value of equity grants — is positioned at the 75th percentile of the same peer group. Pay is benchmarked twice per year using Radford Global Technology Survey data, with a supplemental reference to <URL> for engineering roles to capture real-world market movement in technical talent. When survey data and market anecdotes diverge by more than 10% at a given level, HR initiates an ad hoc market review before the next scheduled calibration. The philosophy is grounded in the belief that compensating people well relative to the market reduces voluntary attrition, eliminates a significant source of distraction, and allows the company to attract and retain people who would otherwise join larger competitors. Career Levels and Pay Bands Apex operates a single unified level framework that applies across all functions — engineering, product, design, sales, finance, HR, legal, and operations. The seven levels and their approximate base salary ranges for Tier 1 locations are: <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Associate: $70,000 to $90,000. Entry-level roles. Typically <DATE_TIME> of professional experience. Supervised closely with structured deliverables. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Specialist: $90,000 to $120,000. Independently executes well-defined tasks. Typically <DATE_TIME> of experience. Contributes to team planning and improves their own work quality without being asked. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Senior: $120,000 to $160,000. Drives outcomes for a defined scope of work with limited oversight. Mentors <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> and <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> colleagues informally. Typically <DATE_TIME> of experience. This is the career level where most employees spend the majority of their tenure. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Staff: $160,000 to $210,000. Owns complex cross-team problems and drives alignment across peers. Influences roadmap and architecture. Typically requires significant domain expertise and strong cross-functional communication. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Principal: $210,000 to $280,000. Sets technical or functional direction for a broad domain. Operates with VP-level influence and visibility. Expected to develop <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> and <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> talent. Fewer than 5% of employees reach this level. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Director: $250,000 to $350,000. Manages teams or functions, sets strategy, represents the domain to senior leadership. Accountable for team performance, hiring, and culture. <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> Vice President: $350,000 and above. Leads a major function or business unit. Member of the senior leadership team. Compensation for <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> is individually negotiated with CEO approval and CFO review. Each band has a published minimum, midpoint, and maximum. The midpoint represents the market-competitive rate for a fully proficient, high-performing employee at that level. Employees newly promoted typically enter between the minimum and the midpoint. SDKs and Client Libraries Official SDKs are maintained by Apex and published under the Apache-2.0 license. Available for Python 3.10 and later, TypeScript 5.0 and later, Java 17 LTS and later, and Go 1.22 and later. Each SDK provides typed models for all request and response objects, automatic retry logic with exponential backoff, transparent token refresh, rate-limit handling, and streaming support for SSE query results. SDK releases are versioned independently from the API. Each SDK documents which API versions it supports. Customers using a supported SDK are notified of API deprecations through the SDK's release notes. Community-maintained SDKs for Ruby and .NET are linked from the developer portal but are not supported by Apex. SDK installation examples: Python: pip install apex-sdk. TypeScript: npm install @apex-systems/sdk. Go: go get <URL> Support and SLA API support is available through the in-product support widget, the customer portal, and a dedicated Slack Connect channel for Enterprise customers. Professional Development Every employee receives an annual learning and development budget of $2,000, effective on their hire anniversary date. The budget may be applied to external conferences, online courses, in-person training, books, professional certifications, or executive coaching. Manager approval is required for individual expenses above $500. The budget does not carry over <DATE_TIME> and cannot be cashed out. Apex sponsors internal knowledge sharing through Friday Tech Talks, a <DATE_TIME> session hosted by a volunteer employee or external speaker every <DATE_TIME> at <DATE_TIME> Pacific. Recordings are posted to the internal learning library. The company holds an annual Engineering Summit in <LOCATION>, which all engineers and product managers are expected to attend regardless of location. Employees pursuing advanced degrees relevant to their role may apply for tuition reimbursement of up to $5,250 per year, the IRS excludable maximum. Applications are reviewed by the HR Business Partner and require evidence of enrollment and a grade of B or better for reimbursement. Code of Conduct Apex is committed to a workplace free from harassment, discrimination, and retaliation. These behaviors are grounds for immediate termination regardless of seniority. Apex Systems Public API — Developer Documentation API version <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> — Revision <DATE_TIME> The Apex Systems Public API enables customers to programmatically ingest data, execute analytical queries, manage users and roles, configure webhooks, and monitor audit events. This document is the primary reference for developers integrating with the <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> API. Base URL and Versioning The <US_DRIVER_LICENSE> API is served at https://api.apex-systems.example/<US_DRIVER_LICENSE>. All HTTPS requests must use TLS 1.2 or higher. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 connections are rejected at the load balancer. Apex uses semantic versioning for the API. Breaking changes — removal of a field, change to an existing field's type or constraints, change in HTTP status code semantics, removal of an endpoint — always increment the major version. A deprecated major version is maintained for a minimum of <DATE_TIME> after the successor version reaches general availability, after which it is sunset with <DATE_TIME> of advance notice via the developer changelog and a deprecation header added to responses. Non-breaking additions — new optional request fields, new response fields, new optional query parameters, new endpoints — may appear in the current version at any time. Clients must be written to tolerate unknown fields in responses. The current API version is included in every response in the X-Apex-Api-Version header. If a client detects that the version in the response differs from the expected version, it should log a warning and test for forward compatibility. Authentication Every API request must include a bearer token in the Authorization HTTP header: Authorization: Bearer {token}. Two token types are supported. API keys are long-lived credentials associated with a service account. They are issued from the Apex administrator console, scoped to one or more roles at issuance time, and remain valid until explicitly revoked. API keys are the recommended authentication method for server-to-server integrations. The key secret is shown exactly once at creation and is not recoverable; if lost, a replacement must be issued and the old key revoked. API keys do not expire automatically but should be rotated on a <DATE_TIME> schedule as a security best practice. OAuth 2.0 access tokens are obtained through the authorization code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange), the recommended flow for interactive applications where the user authenticates with their identity provider. The Apex OIDC discovery document is published at https://auth.apex-systems.example/.well-known/openid-configuration. Access tokens are short-lived with a <DATE_TIME> expiry. Refresh tokens are issued alongside access tokens and are valid for <DATE_TIME>, rotated on each use. Unauthenticated requests receive HTTP 401 Unauthorized with a WWW-Authenticate header. Authenticated requests that lack the required role for the requested operation receive HTTP 403 Forbidden. The error response body follows the standard error model described below. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60 requests in <DATE_TIME>. Ingestion

Customers use Apex to centralize data from disparate source systems, apply compliance-aware transformations, and expose clean, governed datasets to analysts, dashboards, and downstream APIs. The system is organized into four independently operated planes: ingestion, storage, query, and control. Each plane is a set of independently releasable services with its own release cadence, on-call rotation, and reliability budget. Planes communicate exclusively through documented APIs and asynchronous event streams. No direct database access across plane boundaries is permitted. The primary region is us-east-1 and serves the majority of customer traffic. Each region runs an EKS cluster with three dedicated worker node groups. Memory-optimized nodes use r6i.xlarge instances and host the Trino query engine, which requires large JVM heap sizes proportional to query complexity and concurrent user count. Compute-optimized nodes use c6i.2xlarge instances and host the Rust-based ingestion workers, which benefit from high core counts for parallel parsing. Autoscaling is managed by <PERSON>, which provisions and terminates nodes within <DATE_TIME> of a scaling signal. The target utilization is 60% CPU across the node group, providing headroom for burst traffic. Payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256 using the webhook secret. The signature is in the X-Apex-Signature-256 header and is computed over the raw request body. Receivers must be idempotent; the X-Apex-Delivery-Id header provides a stable identifier per event occurrence that can be used for deduplication. Apex retries delivery up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1, <DATE_TIME>, <DATE_TIME>, 125, <DATE_TIME>) if the receiver returns any non-2xx response.
